summary: Small single-storey rectangular building with a stepped brick gable roof, sandstone (light) walls with a wooden base, continuous horizontal windows on all visible sides, corner vertical stone pillars, and a raised light stone (quartz-like) trim / low parapet surrounding the building on the ground plane.

shape.overall_form: simple rectangular box with a central pitched/stepped roof running along the long axis; raised shallow foundation and surrounding low border
shape.symmetry: bilaterally symmetric along the roof ridge (longitudinal symmetry)

materials.name: stonebrick
materials.role: corner pillars / accents (chiseled or smooth darker stone)

materials.name: slab_wood
materials.role: possible interior flooring or small trim under windows (uncertain)

elements: continuous horizontal windows (three panes long per side visible)
central stepped gable roof with slab caps at steps
raised pale border/low parapet around building footprint
rebuild_hints: Footprint: build a rectangular base roughly 9x13 blocks (visible top-down proportion), then inset walls by 1 block from the pale surrounding border.
Walls: use sandstone (smooth/light) for main wall section with a 1-2 block high wooden plank base and stonebrick or darker stone blocks as vertical corner pillars.
Windows: place continuous window openings (3 contiguous glass panes) along each long side centered horizontally with a 1-block high sill of wood or sandstone under them.
Roof: create a stepped gable roof of brick blocks ascending toward the center ridge; cap the step edges with stone slabs (stonebrick/stone slabs) to match visible trim.
Trim: place a continuous pale block (quartz or similar) band around top of wall under the roof and a 1-block-high pale border on the ground around the building footprint.
uncertainties: Exact block types for white trim and ground border: appears to be quartz or smooth stone — identification not certain.
Exact footprint dimensions: estimated from images and top-down perspective; could be off by a few blocks.
Height: internal floor-to-ceiling and any interior partitioning are not visible.
The darker corner blocks are assumed stonebrick/stone; could be another dark stone variant.
Possible interior floor material is uncertain (wood plank inferred from visible lower band).
